mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the history of evolutionary theory including Darwin's natural selection (B.1.b) and the synthetic theory (B.1.d), evidence for evolution from fossils and molecular clues (B.2), the process of evolution through genetic variation and natural selection (C), and speciation (D).

Darwin's theory of natural selection (B.1.b), with adaptation and fitness (C.3.a), natural selection in genetic change (C.3.c), and types of selection including stabilizing, directional, diversifying, and sexual (C.3.d).

Directly covers stages in the emergence of life (A), including hypotheses about the origin of life (A.1), steps in producing chemical precursors (A.2), and the earliest living systems including enzymatic reaction chains and origin of cells (A.3).

Developed the theory of evolution by natural selection (B.1.b), with On the Origin of Species establishing the framework for understanding adaptation, fitness, and the role of natural selection in genetic change (C.3).
Mendel's theory of heredity formalized genetic inheritance (B.1.c), with genetic variation through gene or chromosomal mutation (C.1) and changes in gene frequencies within populations (C.2).
Viruses as key agents of horizontal gene transfer contribute to changes in gene frequencies within populations through gene migration (C.2).

Spans from the RNA world and emergence of protocells (A.3) through evidence of biological activity in the geological record (A.4), with patterns and rates of species evolution including the Cambrian explosion (D.6).

Characterizes biological macromolecules and small molecules relevant to the production of chemical precursors of life (A.2), including organic molecules, long-chain polymers, and the origin of the genetic code.
The geological time scale provides evidence of biological activity in the geological record (A.4), integrating stratigraphic, radiometric, and fossil data.
Reproductive isolation as a criterion for species distinctiveness (D.2), with sexual selection as a type of selection (C.3.d) and the evolutionary paradox of the cost of sex.

Genes as nucleotide sequences connect to the origin of the genetic code (A.2.d), genetic variation through mutation (C.1), changes in gene frequencies (C.2), and genetic differentiation during speciation (D.5).
The evolution of photosynthesis (A.3.c) was a key transition in the earliest living systems, with cyanobacteria responsible for the Great Oxidation Event.
Sexual selection as a type of selection (C.3.d) and sex-determination systems relevant to reproductive isolation as a criterion for species (D.2).
Mass and background extinctions are integral to patterns and rates of species evolution (D.6), shaping the topology of phylogenies.

Mendel's theory of heredity (B.1.c), the synthetic theory integrating genetics and evolution (B.1.d), molecular and genetic studies (B.1.e), the process of evolution through genetic mechanisms (C), and genetic differentiation during speciation (D.5).
Among the earliest life forms (~3.5 Ga), relevant to the origin of procaryotic cells (A.3.b) and horizontal gene transfer as an evolutionary mechanism.
Molecular structures underlying biology connect to molecular and genetic evolutionary studies (B.1.e), molecular clues to evolutionary history (B.2.d), and molecular evolution (D.6.c).
Taxonomy defines species distinctiveness (D.1) and properties of species (D.3), fundamental to understanding speciation and the classification of living things.
Directly covers the origin of species and speciation (D), including species distinctiveness (D.1), reproductive isolation criterion (D.2), properties of species (D.3), and modes of speciation (D.4).
Pasteur experimentally disproved spontaneous generation, directly relevant to hypotheses about the origin of life (A.1) and the scientific understanding of how life emerges.
Carbon's bonding versatility underlies all known life, relevant to the production of simple organic molecules (A.2.b) as chemical precursors of life.
The RNA world hypothesis posits RNA as the primordial molecule, relevant to the origin of the genetic code (A.2.d) and evolution of enzymatic reaction chains (A.3.a).
Proteins as polymers of amino acids relate to production of long-chain molecules (A.2.c), with proteomics providing molecular clues to evolutionary history (B.2.d).

DNA encoding genetic information relates to origin of the genetic code (A.2.d), molecular and genetic studies advancing evolutionary theory (B.1.e), and molecular clues to evolutionary history (B.2.d).
Enzyme catalysis connects to the evolution of enzymatic reaction chains in the earliest living systems (A.3.a), with biocatalysts playing a central role in metabolic evolution.
Flower color, shape, and scent are co-evolutionary adaptations for pollinators, relevant to aspects of natural selection including coloration and mimicry (C.3.b).

Domestication involves multi-generational selection reducing aggression genes and increasing desirable traits, illustrating natural selection processes applied by humans (C.3), though distinct from artificial selection.
The history of evolutionary theory from Lamarck through Darwin, Mendel, and the modern synthesis (B.1) forms a key chapter in the broader history of science.
